options: An acquired thrombotic and hemorrhagic syndrome due to excessive activation of coagulation
s2.explain: DIC is an acquired syndrome characterized by excessive activation of coagulation leading to thrombosis and consumption of clotting factors causing hemorrhage.

s3.q: The clinical picture of DIC includes all EXCEPT:
s3.explain: DIC presents with bleeding from multiple sites (gingival, IV sites, wounds), petechiae, purpura, and multi-organ dysfunction.

s4.q: Common obstetric causes of DIC include all EXCEPT:
s4.explain: Obstetric DIC triggers: placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, severe preeclampsia/HELLP, sepsis, and retained dead fetus.

s5.q: Laboratory findings in DIC include all EXCEPT:
s5.explain: DIC shows thrombocytopenia (low platelets), prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and schistocytes on blood film.

s6.q: The cornerstone of DIC management is:
s6.explain: Treating the underlying obstetric cause is the cornerstone. Blood products (FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets) are given for active bleeding.
